title: iOS Engineer
metaTitle: iOS Engineer Resume Guide | Build a Winning Application
metaDescription: Land your iOS Engineer role with a resume that highlights Swift, Xcode, and app deployment. See sample bullets and ATS keywords that work.
h1: iOS Engineer Resume: How to Stand Out to Hiring Managers
intro: iOS engineering roles go to candidates who can prove they've shipped features users love—not just written code. This guide shows you how to build a resume that gets past ATS systems and impresses technical interviewers.
audience: Recent computer science grads, mid-level iOS developers looking to switch companies, and engineers transitioning from Android or web to native iOS development.

topSkills.name: Swift
topSkills.why: Nearly every iOS job posting lists Swift as a core requirement; it's the modern standard and shows you can write clean, contemporary iOS code.

topSkills.name: UIKit / SwiftUI
topSkills.why: Hiring managers need to know you can build user interfaces—UIKit for legacy codebases and SwiftUI for modern greenfield projects.

topSkills.name: Xcode
topSkills.why: The IDE is non-negotiable; it signals you can navigate builds, debug, and profile apps without handholding.

topSkills.name: REST APIs & JSON
topSkills.why: Most iOS apps are client-side; demonstrating API integration experience shows you understand the full stack.

topSkills.name: Core Data / SQLite
topSkills.why: Local data persistence is a foundation skill; companies need engineers who can design and query databases on-device.

topSkills.name: Git & Version Control
topSkills.why: Teams expect you to collaborate via branches, merges, and pull requests without friction.

topSkills.name: Networking & Concurrency
topSkills.why: URLSession, GCD, Async/await—recruiters want evidence you can handle asynchronous tasks and network calls safely.

topSkills.name: TestFlight & App Store Deployment
topSkills.why: Shipping to production is a differentiator; mention experience with release builds, versioning, and app store review cycles.

topSkills.name: MVVM / MVC Architecture
topSkills.why: Code structure matters; showing you understand design patterns demonstrates maturity and maintainability mindset.

topSkills.name: Debugging & Performance Optimization
topSkills.why: Instruments, profiling, and crash reporting experience shows you care about app quality and user experience beyond feature velocity.

sampleBullets.weak: Developed iOS app features using Swift and Xcode
sampleBullets.strong: Built 4 major product features (payment checkout, push notifications, offline sync) in Swift, reducing app crashes by 35% through systematic memory profiling and Core Data optimization
sampleBullets.lesson: Add specificity (which features), quantify impact (crash reduction %), and mention tools/frameworks to show depth and measurable outcomes.

sampleBullets.weak: Collaborated with product and design teams to deliver app updates
sampleBullets.strong: Partnered with product and design to ship 12 monthly releases; coordinated with QA to reduce post-launch bugs by 40% via automated unit and UI tests covering 65% of codebase
sampleBullets.lesson: Replace 'collaborated' with concrete shipping velocity, tie collaboration to quality metrics, and call out testing practices that prove reliability.

sampleBullets.weak: Improved app performance and fixed bugs
sampleBullets.strong: Refactored image caching layer using NSCache and lazy loading, cutting app launch time from 8s to 3.2s; diagnosed and resolved 18 high-priority bugs in UIKit stack views via Xcode debugger
sampleBullets.lesson: Name specific bottlenecks and tools, show before/after numbers, and quantify bug fixes to demonstrate problem-solving rigor.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing languages like 'Objective-C' without context if you only learned it in school
commonMistakes.fix: Only include Objective-C if you've shipped code or contributed to a living codebase; otherwise, focus on Swift and modern frameworks.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to mention app release history or metrics
commonMistakes.fix: Lead with shipped projects: include app name, download count, or user base (e.g., '200K+ downloads on App Store') to prove real-world impact.

commonMistakes.mistake: Vague descriptions of 'building apps' without naming architecture or libraries
commonMistakes.fix: Be explicit: 'Architected music streaming feature using MVVM, URLSession, and Combine' beats 'developed app features.'
Call out unit testing, integration testing, and CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, Fastlane, etc.) to show you ship quality code consistently.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not mentioning app store deployment or release processes
commonMistakes.fix: Include TestFlight beta testing, app signing, versioning strategy, and app store submission experience—it's a real skill that separates juniors from pros.

structureTips: Lead your experience section with shipped iOS projects or products; put app name, role, and core impact in the first line, then drill into features and metrics.
Group technical skills into tiers: Proficient (Swift, UIKit/SwiftUI, Xcode) separate from Familiar (Objective-C, Kotlin) so hiring managers immediately see depth.
Highlight testing, debugging, and performance work in bullets, not hidden in a catch-all; these signal engineering maturity and reduce hiring risk.
If you have open-source contributions or a portfolio of shipped apps, link them prominently or use a 'Projects' section before Experience to showcase live proof.
salarySignal: Entry-level iOS Engineers in the US typically earn $100K–$130K in 2026; mid-level roles range from $130K–$180K, with senior positions exceeding $180K at top tech companies.

faq.q: Should I put Objective-C on my iOS Engineer resume if I learned it years ago?
faq.a: Only if you've shipped production code with it or worked on a live legacy app. Otherwise, lead with Swift and modern frameworks to show you're current. If you do mention it, note the year or context (e.g., 'Maintained Objective-C codebase for 2 years before migration to Swift').

faq.q: How do I talk about an app I built solo or in a bootcamp without shipping numbers?
faq.a: Focus on features and technical depth: 'Built note-taking app with SwiftUI, Core Data persistence, CloudKit sync, and 40+ unit tests.' Link the GitHub repo or App Store link so reviewers can see it live.

faq.q: Do I need to mention both UIKit and SwiftUI, or just SwiftUI?
faq.a: Mention both if you've used both. Many codebases still use UIKit; newer ones use SwiftUI. Saying you're proficient in both makes you more hireable. If you've only used one, be honest—but prioritize SwiftUI for modern roles.

faq.q: How important is open-source contribution for an iOS Engineer resume?
faq.a: It's a nice-to-have, not required. If you have a solid shipped product or strong portfolio, prioritize that. Open-source shows initiative and community engagement, so include it if you have 3+ meaningful PRs or an active repo.

faq.q: What's the best way to show app performance improvements on my resume?
faq.a: Use before-and-after metrics: 'Optimized image loading with NSCache, reducing app launch time from 8s to 3.2s' or 'Eliminated memory leaks in background sync, cutting app crashes by 35%.' Mention the tool (Instruments, Xcode profiler) to prove you measured it.
